title: Pakistani Nihari (Home Style)

ingredients.name: onion, thinly sliced and deep-fried until golden (birista)
nihari masala or meat masala blend
visual_checks: MDH Nihari Masala from Indian grocery stores
ingredients.notes: MDH Nihari Masala from Indian grocery stores

subs.name: 2 tbsp homemade spice mix: 1 tsp each cumin, coriander, fennel plus 0.5 tsp each ginger powder, clove, cardamom

steps.text: Heat oil or ghee in a large heavy-bottomed pot over high heat. Add the mutton shanks and sear well on all sides until brown, about 8 minutes. Remove the meat and set aside.

In the same pot, add ginger-garlic paste and fry for 2 minutes until the raw smell goes. Add the nihari masala, red chilli powder, coriander powder, turmeric, fennel seeds, and bay leaves. Stir on medium heat for 1 minute.

Return the meat to the pot. Crush in about three-quarters of the fried onions. Pour in 1.2 litres of hot water, add salt, bring to a boil, then reduce heat to the lowest setting. Cover tightly and simmer for 90 minutes, checking occasionally and skimming any foam from the surface.

Mix the wheat flour with 5 tablespoons of cold water into a smooth slurry with no lumps. Ladle a cup of hot broth from the pot into the slurry while whisking, then pour the mixture back into the pot. Stir well to prevent lumps.

Continue simmering uncovered for 20 more minutes, stirring every few minutes, until the stew has thickened to a velvety consistency that coats the back of a spoon. Add garam masala and taste for salt.

Ladle into deep bowls. Top each serving with ginger julienne, sliced green chillies, a squeeze of lemon juice, the remaining crispy fried onions, and fresh coriander. Serve with naan for dipping into the broth.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mutton → 800 g beef shin or brisket (increase simmer time to 2.5 hours); curry-powder → 2 tbsp homemade spice mix: 1 tsp each cumin, coriander, fennel plus 0.5 tsp each ginger powder, clove, cardamom (if nihari masala not available); wheat-flour → 2 tbsp rice flour or cornflour (for a gluten-free thickener)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a pressure cooker to cut the simmering time dramatically: after step 3, pressure cook for 8–10 whistles on medium heat instead of the 90-minute simmer. Release pressure naturally, then open and continue with the flour slurry on direct heat. MDH Nihari Masala is widely available at Indian spice shops. If unavailable, use Shan Nihari Masala or mix meat masala with a pinch of kewra water (gulab jal) and some extra fennel.
